text: This video presents a robotic rejection of a central liver solitary fibrous tumor.
This is the case of a thirty-year-old patient without medical history.
The patient presented upper abdominal pain.
A CT scan and MRI were performed and revealed an eight-centimeter tumor in the left liver, invading segment I and medial hepatic vein.
A pathologic exam revealed a solitary fibrous tumor, and a robotic left hepatectomy extended to caudate lobe and median hepatic vein was planned.
On preoperative MRI with gadolinam injection in T-I sequence, we can see an heterogeneous hyper-oscular tumor located above the glycineal division, and evading Sandman-I and median hepatic vein.

The patient is positioned supine with legs apart, while the surgical robot is docked from the head. Four robotic ports are utilized: one in the supra-umbilical position, two in the left hypochondrium, and one in the right hypochondrium.
The surgeon performs dissection around the supra-hepatic vein using robotic instruments, carefully separating the surrounding tissue.
The robotic arm lowers the hepatic dome, providing a stable exposure of the supra-hepatic veins.
The surgeon completes a cholecystectomy, removing the gallbladder from its attachments.
The surgeon sections the cystic artery and cystic duct between Hem-o-lok clips, completing the division of these structures.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect the hepatic pedicle, with the robotic arm providing stable exposure of the area.
The robotic arm four assists in exposing the surgical field, providing a clear view of the supra-hepatic veins.
The surgeon identifies hepatic arterial branches, visible within the stable exposition provided by the robotic arm lowering the hepatic dome.
The surgeon identifies the left hepatic arterial branch, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the arterial branch for segment four, using robotic assistance for exposure.
The surgeon sectionates the hepatic arterial branches between Hem-o-lok clips, ensuring hemostasis by securely clamping the vessels.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the left portal branch, using robotic assistance for exposure.
The surgeon sections the identified hepatic arterial branches between hemlock clips to ensure hemostasis.
The surgeon releases the inferior vena cava, freeing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon sectionates the right part of the caudate lobe, ensuring precise separation of the liver tissue.
One fold is helpful for exposure.
The surgeon sectionates the accessory infra-hepatic veins between hemlock clips, completing the dissection of these vessels.
The surgeon marks the parenchymal transection line with monopolar coagulation in the anterior sector of the right side of the medial hepatic vein, following ultrasonographic anatomic analysis.
The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using a robotic hook, bipolar forceps, ultrasonic dissector, and vacuum irrigator, manipulating the liver tissue with precision.

The surgeon irrigates the bipolar forceps to enhance the efficiency of bipolar energy, preventing coagulated tissue from sticking and tearing fragile vessels, while maintaining a clear view of the forceps' tips.
The segment V vein is visible, draining into the median hepatic vein.
The surgeon sections the accessory infra-hepatic veins between hemlock clips, completing the transection of these vessels.
The surgeon extends the parenchymal resection in the anterior sector for tumoral resection, following the previously marked transection line near the medial hepatic vein.
The left hepatic duct is visible in the surgical field, identifiable as a tubular structure within the liver anatomy.
Indocyanine green injection is administered, enhancing the visualization of biliary convergence through fluorescence.
The surgeon dissects and controls the left hepatic duct following a chemo-liver transection, ensuring the right biliary structures remain unharmed.
The surgeon places hematoclips on the left hepatic duct, securing it after dissection.
The surgeon removes the tumor from the upper part of the portal division, completing the resection process.
The surgeon dissects the caudate lobe, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon releases the inferior vena cava, freeing it from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses an arm form to expose the anterior part of the inferior vena cava, revealing its position and surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses robotic bipolar and hook instruments to facilitate dissection control and negate accessory suprahepatic veins, ensuring precise manipulation and control of these vascular structures.
Two bipolar forceps are used to expose the surgical area and achieve hemostasis along the transection line, ensuring clear visibility and control during the procedure.
The venous branch from segment VIII is visualized draining into the median hepatic vein and is sectioned between hemlock clips.
The surgeon completes the parenchymal transection, revealing the inferior vena cava clearly in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects segment four and the superior part of segment one from the inferior vena cava, carefully separating these liver segments from the large vein.
The surgeon controls, coagulates, and sections the left part of the retroclobal ligament using surgical instruments.
The surgeon controls the median hepatic vein, using a loop to facilitate exposure, and sections it between hematoclips.
The surgeon controls and exposes the left hepatic vein, then sections it using a vascular stapler.
The surgeon sectionates the left triangular ligament, followed by the removal of the specimen through an incision in the under-back area.
The surgeon displays the transection line marking the completion of the operation, showing the final separation of tissue planes.
The operation lasted for three hundred and thirty minutes. Estimated blood loss was one hundred and fifty milliliters. No blood transfusion was needed.
During a post-operative course, patient developed biliary fistula, dried up at post-operative day twenty, but it was discharged on post-operative day eight.
Pathological exam confirmed solitary fibrous tumor, restricted completely.
Patient was free from disease after three months.
